intro: Porting C++ routines to Bash shifts logic from compiled, highly performant type-safe systems to interpreted Unix shell scripts designed for process orchestration. Bash strips away the static overhead, compilers, and structural safety of C++ in favor of string-based environment interaction and command pipeline manipulation. This migration drastically simplifies infrastructure tasks and filesystem operations while sacrificing computational speed.

diffs.from: Static, rigidly enforced nominal system with complex abstract classes, compile-time templates, and explicit memory alignments.
diffs.to: Untyped system where all variables are internally handled as text strings, with explicit context-driven integer evaluation flags.

diffs.from: Strict token-based syntax utilizing curly braces, terminators, headers, and extensive namespace configurations.
diffs.to: Command-line driven syntax prioritizing whitespace separation, stream redirection structures, and reliance on external binaries.

diffs.from: Granular execution over execution stacks, heap segments, explicit reference structures, and RAII tracking scopes.
diffs.to: Abstracted process environment allocations managed automatically by the shell host process lifetime and subshell contexts.

diffs.from: Native multi-threading APIs, execution policies, locks, and shared memory mapping utilities.
diffs.to: Process-level concurrency achieved using background workers (&), job controls, wait primitives, and named pipes.

diffs.from: The STL delivers algorithmic foundations, generic collection primitives, and system io stream handles.
diffs.to: Relies completely on standard POSIX command utilities (sed, awk, grep, find) and operating system pipeline pipes.

examples.title: File Existence Verification
examples.description: Compares checking if a file exists using C++17 filesystem libraries vs a native Bash flag verification.

examples.title: Subprocess Loop Capture
examples.description: Demonstrates invoking a system command and processing lines in C++ via pipes versus native Bash command substitution.
examples.toCode:
for file in $(ls); do
  echo "$file"
done

tips.title: Always Quote Variable References
tips.body: Bash performs word splitting and globbing on unquoted variables. Always wrap expressions in double quotes (e.g., `"$VAR"`) to handle whitespace safely, unlike C++ type-bound strings.

tips.title: Check Process Exit Statuses
tips.body: Bash does not throw exceptions. Interrogate the `$?` variable immediately after an operation or use `set -e` at the top of scripts to automatically exit on unexpected runtime command failures.

tips.title: Replace Vectors with Core Arrays
tips.body: For basic listing, use Bash indexed arrays (`declare -a arr`) and associative arrays (`declare -A map`). Avoid string accumulation hacks when managing multi-item sets.

tips.title: Delegate Math to External Tools
tips.body: Bash performs basic integer arithmetic only via `$((expression))`. If your C++ code involves floating-point numbers or advanced equations, pipe the logic to `bc` or `awk` inside the script.
